one_line_takeaway: Anchored E-Watermarking replaces ad hoc p-value/fixed-horizon LLM watermark detection with a provably worst-case-log-growth-optimal e-value detector that enables valid anytime early stopping, cutting median detection token budget from 145 (best non-anchored baseline) to 72 tokens on MarkMyWords while matching or beating baseline text quality.
key_finding: On the MarkMyWords real-model benchmark (Table 1, median over private keys and perturbation methods, alpha=0.02): the paper's Theorem 4.1 e-value scheme achieves Quality 0.919 and Size 72.0 tokens, versus baselines Exponential (0.907, size infinity -- over half of watermarked generations undetected after perturbation), Inverse Transform (0.917, 734.0), Binary (0.919, size infinity), Distribution Shift/green-red-list (0.912, 145.0), and SEAL (0.901, 84.5). The paper highlights an approximately 2x reduction in detection token budget versus the best non-anchored baseline (Distribution Shift: 145 -> 72.0 tokens) and improvement over the anchor-based SEAL baseline (84.5 -> 72.0), at equal or better quality than all baselines. The abstract's aggregate figure of a '13-15% relative' reduction in average token budget versus state-of-the-art baselines across temperature settings is stated in the abstract/intro but the corroborating per-temperature table is deferred to Appendix D, which is not included in the first 22 pages extracted here.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (distinct from the Related Work it surveys) is 'Anchored E-Watermarking': the first e-value-based framework for statistical LLM watermarking. Given a shared 'anchor' reference distribution p0 (e.g., a smaller open-source LLM) that the target model's distribution q is assumed to lie within an L1 ball of radius delta around, the authors (1) formulate detection as a robust log-optimality problem over e-values (Problem 3.1) rather than classical p-values, (2) derive in closed form the worst-case-log-growth-optimal e-value e*(v,s) and the optimal generator coupling w* (Theorem 4.1), (3) prove this same e-value is also optimal for the sequential/anytime-valid stopping-time problem against an adaptive adversary who can vary the target distribution at every step (Theorem 4.3, matching lower and upper bounds on sample complexity), and (4) empirically validate the theory with synthetic two-token simulations and a real-model watermarking benchmark. The paper explicitly notes that the SEAL method (Huang et al., 2025) already uses the optimal *generator* coupling (their Eq. 4 coincides with SEAL's speculative-decoding coupling) but SEAL's *detector* is not optimal -- this paper supplies the missing, provably-optimal detection rule.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker-defender security paper in the classical sense; it is a statistical-detection-theory paper for provenance. The 'adversary' formalized in Problem 3.3 is a distributional adversary A that adaptively selects the target distribution q_t within the delta-neighborhood of the anchor at each generation step (modeling worst-case, potentially non-i.i.d./autoregressive text and, per the Discussion, 'adaptive attacks' where an attacker perturbs text after some tokens are generated). The paper does not model text-editing/paraphrasing/removal attacks directly in its theory -- those are only probed empirically via the MarkMyWords benchmark's built-in perturbation suite (character-, word-, and text-level edits, including paraphrasing and round-trip translation). No adversarial ML attack (e.g., watermark stripping, spoofing, forgery) is separately analyzed or claimed as a contribution.
limitations: Self-reported/evident limitations: (1) the theory assumes q lies within a known L1 ball of radius delta around a shared anchor p0 that both generator and detector access -- validity depends on this proximity assumption holding in practice, and the paper offers no procedure for calibrating delta from data; (2) the delta-ball/finite-alphabet analysis is proven for a discrete token-level model and the closed-form optimum (Theorem 4.1) is derived under a fixed cardinality n=|V|; (3) the sample-complexity result (Theorem 4.3) covers only an adversary that selects the *target distribution* adaptively within the anchor neighborhood, not adversaries who edit/remove/paraphrase already-generated text -- robustness to those (the practically dominant threat) is assessed only empirically via MarkMyWords perturbations, not proven; (4) the paper states future work includes 'extension to more flexible anchor distributions' and 'game-theoretic implications ... against incentivized adversaries,' implicitly conceding the current adversary model is narrow; (5) synthetic verification of the theory is restricted to the two-token (|V|=2) case for tractability of the numerical solver.

datasets_used: MarkMyWords (Piet et al., 2023) benchmark: 300 generations across book summarization, creative writing, and news article generation tasks
quantitative_results: Theorem 4.1 scheme: Quality 0.919, Size 72.0 tokens (median, alpha=0.02)
Distribution Shift baseline: Quality 0.912, Size 145.0 tokens
Inverse Transform baseline: Quality 0.917, Size 734.0 tokens
Exponential baseline: Quality 0.907, Size = infinity (over half of watermarked generations undetected after perturbation)

Abstract-level claim: 13-15% relative reduction in average token budget vs. state-of-the-art baselines across temperature settings (full breakdown deferred to Appendix D, not verified in extracted text)
Closed-form optimal log-growth rate J* = H(p0) + (1-delta/2)log(1-delta/2) + (delta/2)log(delta/(2(n-1)))
